annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of arithmetic

anchor.snippet: states that every integer greater than 1 is either prime or can be represented uniquely as a product of prime numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib states the FTA as uniqueness of the prime factor list (`Nat.primeFactorsList_unique`) together with `Nat.prod_primeFactorsList` for existence.

annotations.label: Prime factorization of 1200

anchor.snippet: there will always be exactly four 2s, one 3, two 5s, and no other primes in the product

annotations.note: Specific numerical example (1200 = 2^4·3·5^2) is not stated as a lemma in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Necessity of prime factors

anchor.snippet: The requirement that the factors be prime is necessary

annotations.note: This is a meta-remark about the theorem's hypotheses rather than a formal statement.

annotations.label: Restatement using empty product convention

anchor.snippet: every positive integer can be represented uniquely as a product of prime numbers, up to the order of the factors

annotations.note: The same theorem with the empty-product convention is captured by `Nat.primeFactorsList_unique` (the case `n=1` yields the empty list).

annotations.label: Why 1 is not prime

anchor.snippet: if 1 were prime, then factorization into primes would not be unique

annotations.note: An informal justification for the convention; Mathlib's `Nat.Prime` simply excludes 1 by definition.

annotations.label: Generalization to UFDs

anchor.snippet: The theorem generalizes to other algebraic structures that are called unique factorization domains

annotations.note: `UniqueFactorizationMonoid` axiomatizes UFD-style existence and uniqueness of factorizations into irreducibles.

annotations.label: Euclid's lemma (Elements VII.30)

anchor.snippet: if a prime p divides the product ab , then p divides either a or b or both

annotations.note: Euclid's lemma is `Nat.Prime.dvd_mul : p.Prime → (p ∣ m * n ↔ p ∣ m ∨ p ∣ n)`.

annotations.label: Every composite has a prime divisor (Elements VII.31)

anchor.snippet: every integer greater than one is divided evenly by some prime number

annotations.note: `Nat.exists_prime_and_dvd : n ≠ 1 → ∃ p, Prime p ∧ p ∣ n` is precisely Elements VII.31.

annotations.label: Every number is prime or has a prime divisor (Elements VII.32)

anchor.snippet: Proposition 32 is derived from proposition 31, and proves that the decomposition is possible

annotations.note: Existence of a prime factorization is given by `Nat.prod_primeFactorsList` together with `Nat.prime_of_mem_primeFactorsList`.

annotations.label: LCM of primes (Elements IX.14)

anchor.snippet: a least common multiple of several prime numbers is not a multiple of any other prime number

annotations.note: The general factorization-of-LCM lemma exists; the exact Elements IX.14 phrasing is not isolated.

annotations.label: Canonical representation

anchor.section: Canonical representation of a positive integer
anchor.snippet: Every positive integer n > 1 can be represented in exactly one way as a product of prime powers

annotations.note: `Nat.factorization n : ℕ →₀ ℕ` records the canonical prime-power exponent function, with `prod_factorization_pow_eq_self` recovering `n`.

annotations.label: Canonical representations of 999, 1000, 1001

anchor.section: Canonical representation of a positive integer

annotations.note: Specific numerical factorizations of 999/1000/1001 are not stated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Infinite product representation

anchor.section: Canonical representation of a positive integer
anchor.snippet: any positive integer can be uniquely represented as an infinite product taken over all the positive prime numbers

annotations.note: `Nat.factorization` is a finitely-supported function on all primes, which is the Mathlib version of this 'infinite product with finitely many nonzero exponents'.

annotations.label: Canonical form for positive rationals

anchor.section: Canonical representation of a positive integer
anchor.snippet: Allowing negative exponents provides a canonical form for positive rational numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define a `Rat.factorization : ℕ →₀ ℤ` canonical form; only `padicValRat` gives individual exponents.

annotations.label: Product, GCD, LCM via canonical representations

anchor.snippet: can be expressed simply in terms of the canonical representations of a and b themselves

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Nat.factorization_mul`, `Nat.factorization_gcd`, and `Nat.factorization_lcm` expressing these operations on factorizations.

annotations.label: Additive and multiplicative functions determined by prime powers

anchor.snippet: the values of additive and multiplicative functions are determined by their values on the powers of prime numbers

annotations.note: `Nat.multiplicative_factorization` (and `Nat.recOnPrimeCoprime` induction principle) encodes that a multiplicative function is determined by its values on prime powers.

annotations.label: Proof uses Euclid's lemma

anchor.snippet: If a prime divides the product of two integers, then it must divide at least one of these integers

annotations.note: Same statement as Euclid's lemma, formalized as `Nat.Prime.dvd_mul`.

annotations.label: Existence of prime factorization

anchor.snippet: It must be shown that every integer greater than 1 is either prime or a product of primes

annotations.note: Existence is `Nat.prod_primeFactorsList : n ≠ 0 → (primeFactorsList n).prod = n` with primality of the entries by `Nat.prime_of_mem_primeFactorsList`.

annotations.label: Uniqueness of prime factorization

anchor.snippet: Suppose, to the contrary, there is an integer that has two distinct prime factorizations

annotations.note: Uniqueness up to permutation is `Nat.primeFactorsList_unique`.

annotations.label: Uniqueness without Euclid's lemma

anchor.snippet: The fundamental theorem of arithmetic can also be proved without using Euclid's lemma

annotations.note: An alternative proof strategy; Mathlib's proof of `primeFactorsList_unique` does use Euclid's lemma.

annotations.label: Gaussian integers have unique factorization

anchor.snippet: the composites have unique factorization as a product of primes

annotations.note: Mathlib gives ℤ[i] a `EuclideanDomain` instance, which induces UFD via the PID-to-UFD chain, but no explicit `UniqueFactorizationMonoid ℤ[i]` lemma is stated.

annotations.label: Eisenstein integers have unique factorization

anchor.snippet: This is the ring of Eisenstein integers , and he proved it has the six units

annotations.note: Mathlib has no ring of Eisenstein integers ℤ[ω]; the existing Eisenstein files concern Eisenstein's criterion and Eisenstein series.

annotations.label: Failure of unique factorization

anchor.snippet: it was also discovered that unique factorization does not always hold

annotations.note: A historical/illustrative remark; Mathlib does not state a counterexample like ℤ[√-5] as a named lemma.

annotations.label: Irreducible vs prime

anchor.snippet: In algebraic number theory 2 is called irreducible

annotations.note: Both `Irreducible` and `Prime` are defined in `Mathlib.Algebra.Prime.Defs`.

annotations.label: Prime implies irreducible in integral domains

anchor.snippet: Using these definitions it can be proven that in any integral domain a prime must be irreducible

annotations.note: `Prime.irreducible : Prime p → Irreducible p` is proved in `Mathlib.Algebra.Prime.Defs`.

annotations.label: Unique factorization domain

anchor.snippet: The rings in which factorization into irreducibles is essentially unique are called unique factorization domains

annotations.note: `UniqueFactorizationMonoid` axiomatizes exactly this notion.

annotations.label: Dedekind domain

anchor.snippet: the rings in which they have unique factorization are called Dedekind domains

annotations.note: `IsDedekindDomain` is defined and `Ideal.uniqueFactorizationMonoid` proves ideals factor uniquely.

annotations.label: Unique factorization for ordinals

anchor.snippet: There is a version of unique factorization for ordinals

annotations.note: Mathlib has Cantor Normal Form for ordinals (`Ordinal.CNF`), which is the closest analog; the specific multiplicative unique factorization theorem is not stated.

annotations.label: Unique factorization in commutative Möbius monoids

anchor.snippet: Any commutative Möbius monoid satisfies a unique factorization theorem

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of 'Möbius monoid'; the `Moebius.lean` file in `UniqueFactorizationDomain` is about the Möbius function on UFDs, not Möbius monoids.
